chief_complaint: I have hives and itching but I can breathe fine

note.soap:
S:
The patient is a 34-year-old adult presenting with hives and itching. They report no difficulty breathing, no fainting, and no fever. There are no known medication allergies.

O:
No physical exam or vital signs available due to phone-only encounter. No evidence of respiratory distress or systemic symptoms reported.

A:
 - Working diagnosis (most likely): Acute urticaria (hives) without anaphylaxis
 - Differential:
  1. Allergic urticaria
  2. Idiopathic urticaria
  3. Viral exanthem presenting with urticaria
  4. Contact dermatitis with urticarial reaction
 - Red flags considered:
  - No breathing difficulty or airway compromise
  - No syncope or fainting
  - No fever or systemic signs of infection

P:
 - Disposition: Manage as outpatient with close follow-up if symptoms worsen
 - Plan:
   Medication protocol (from data/protocols/meds):
   - Medication recommendations (Tier 1):
   - Reviewing physician will confirm appropriateness and prescribe if indicated.
   - Supportive care:
   - Avoid triggers: Stop any new suspected trigger (food/supplement/med) if safe to do so; avoid hot showers which can worsen itching.
   - Skin care: Cool compresses; fragrance-free moisturizer; avoid scratching.
   - Follow-up and safety:
   - Re-evaluation: If hives persist >24–48h, recur frequently, or you need frequent medication dosing—seek in-person evaluation.
   - Urgent/ED now: Any trouble breathing, throat tightness, swelling of lips/tongue/face, fainting, severe dizziness, or widespread blistering rash—call 911/go to ED.
   - Recommend oral antihistamines such as cetirizine 10 mg once daily or loratadine 10 mg once daily to control itching and hives.
   - Avoid known triggers if identified.
   - Monitor for any signs of respiratory difficulty, swelling of face or throat, or systemic symptoms.
 - Safety net:
   - Advise patient to seek emergency care immediately if they develop difficulty breathing, swelling of the face or throat, dizziness, or fainting.
   - Follow up with primary care or urgent care if hives persist beyond several days or worsen despite treatment.
patient_instructions: Avoid triggers: Stop any new suspected trigger (food/supplement/med) if safe to do so; avoid hot showers which can worsen itching.
Skin care: Cool compresses; fragrance-free moisturizer; avoid scratching.
Re-evaluation: If hives persist >24–48h, recur frequently, or you need frequent medication dosing—seek in-person evaluation.
Urgent/ED now: Any trouble breathing, throat tightness, swelling of lips/tongue/face, fainting, severe dizziness, or widespread blistering rash—call 911/go to ED.
